request.question: Mick Jackson and Drew Renaud, have which mutual occupation?

title: Yuri Nosenko: Double Agent
Mick Jackson (director)
Mick Jackson (author)
Denial (2016 film)
Volcano (1997 film)
Chattahoochee (film)
Drew Renaud
'Igginbottom
Torrente 3: El protector
Covert One: The Hades Factor
sentences: Yuri Nosenko: Double Agent is a 1986 British television drama produced by the BBC in association with Primetime Television Ltd. and directed by Mick Jackson.
Mick Jackson (born 4 October 1943 in Grays, England, United Kingdom) is a British film director and television producer.
Between 1973 and 1987, Jackson directed many documentary and drama productions for BBC TV and Channel 4.
Relocating to Hollywood, he directed feature films, including "The Bodyguard" starring Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.
In 2010, Jackson won an Emmy for Outstanding Directing for a Miniseries, Movie or a Dramatic Special for the dramatised biographical TV film "Temple Grandin".
Mick Jackson (born 1960) is a British writer from England, best known for his novel "The Underground Man" (1997).
The book, based on the life of William Cavendish-Scott-Bentinck, 5th Duke of Portland, was shortlisted for the Man Booker Prize and for the 1997 Whitbread Award for best first novel.
Denial is a 2016 historical drama film directed by Mick Jackson and written by David Hare, based on Deborah Lipstadt's book "History on Trial: My Day in Court with a Holocaust Denier."
It dramatises the "Irving v Penguin Books Ltd" case, in which Lipstadt, a Holocaust scholar, was sued by Holocaust denier David Irving for libel.
It stars Rachel Weisz, Tom Wilkinson, Timothy Spall, Andrew Scott, Jack Lowden, Caren Pistorius and Alex Jennings.
Volcano is a 1997 American disaster film directed by Mick Jackson and produced by Andrew Z. Davis, Neal H. Moritz and Lauren Shuler Donner.
The storyline was conceived from a screenplay written by Jerome Armstrong and Billy Ray.
The film features Tommy Lee Jones, Anne Heche, and Don Cheadle.
Jones is cast as the head of the Los Angeles County Office of Emergency Management (LAC OEM) which has complete authority in the event of an emergency or natural disaster.
His character attempts to divert the path of a dangerous lava flow through the streets of Los Angeles following the formation of a volcano at the La Brea Tar Pits.
Chattahoochee is a 1989 American drama film directed by Mick Jackson and starring Gary Oldman and Dennis Hopper.
The film is based on the real-life experiences of Chris Calhoun, who met screenwriter James Hicks, who then wrote a script based on his internment in a Florida state mental institution.

It was turned down by several major studios before being accepted by Hemdale Film Corporation, a small British-owned, Los Angeles-based company that also produced "Platoon", "Hoosiers", "The Last Emperor", and "Salvador".
Michael "Drew" Renaud (born 1985) is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer.
He has received awards from the San Diego Film Festival and LA Shorts Fest.
'Igginbottom were an English progressive rock band, featuring Steven Robinson (guitar), Allan Holdsworth (guitar, vocals), Dave Freeman (drums) and Mick Skelly (bass).
They were managed by a company called Mimo, which was owned by Mick Jackson, Mo Bacon, Ronnie Scott and his business partner Pete King.
Their only album, "'Igginbottom's Wrench", was released in 1969.
Torrente 3: El protector is a 2005 Spanish black comedy film directed, written, produced and starring Santiago Segura as the lead character, Spanish cop José Luis Torrente.
The film was the third in the popular Torrente series (following ) and was successful at the box office.
Santiago Segura decided to make a parody of the American film "The Bodyguard", which was directed by Mick Jackson and starred Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.
Covert One: The Hades Factor (a.k.a. "Robert Ludlum's Covert One: The Hades Factor", "The Hades Factor") is a made-for-TV thriller filmed in Toronto that first aired in 2006.
Directed by Mick Jackson, the miniseries is loosely based on "The Hades Factor", a 2000 novel written by Gayle Lynds as part of the Covert-One series created by Robert Ludlum.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Mick Jackson and Drew Renaud, have which mutual occupation?
hop1_docs: Drew Renaud | Michael "Drew" Renaud (born 1985) is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer.  He has received awards from the San Diego Film Festival and LA Shorts Fest.

Mick Jackson (singer) | Michael George "Mick" Jackson (born 2 November 1947) is an English singer-songwriter and the co-writer of the song, "Blame It on the Boogie".  The song was co-authored by Mick's brother David Jackson and Elmar Krohn, and produced by Sylvester Levay.

Mick Jackson (director) | Mick Jackson (born 4 October 1943 in Grays, England, United Kingdom) is a British film director and television producer.  Between 1973 and 1987, Jackson directed many documentary and drama productions for BBC TV and Channel 4.  Relocating to Hollywood, he directed feature films, including "The Bodyguard" starring Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.  In 2010, Jackson won an Emmy for Outstanding Directing for a Miniseries, Movie or a Dramatic Special for the dramatised biographical TV film "Temple Grandin".

Hernani Almeida | Hernani Almeida (born 7 April 1978 in Sao Vicente, Cape Verde) is a Cape Verdean musician, having started at age 7 his musical career via a small keyboard switching at the age of 14 to the electric guitar, an instrument that became his brand image.  After 1989 he joined the group's musical Mick Lima appears in 1994 with his rock band named What, which drew the attention as a guitarist of the music greats of Cape Verde.

Mick Jackson (author) | Mick Jackson (born 1960) is a British writer from England, best known for his novel "The Underground Man" (1997).  The book, based on the life of William Cavendish-Scott-Bentinck, 5th Duke of Portland, was shortlisted for the Man Booker Prize and for the 1997 Whitbread Award for best first novel.

'Igginbottom | 'Igginbottom were an English progressive rock band, featuring Steven Robinson (guitar), Allan Holdsworth (guitar, vocals), Dave Freeman (drums) and Mick Skelly (bass).  They were managed by a company called Mimo, which was owned by Mick Jackson, Mo Bacon, Ronnie Scott and his business partner Pete King.  Their only album, "'Igginbottom's Wrench", was released in 1969.

The Comforters | The Comforters is the first novel by Scottish author Muriel Spark.  She drew on experiences as a recent convert to Catholicism and having suffered hallucinations due to using Dexedrine, an amphetamine then available over the counter for dieting.  Although completed in late 1955, the book was not published until 1957.  A mutual friend, novelist Alan Barnsley, had sent the proofs to Evelyn Waugh.  At the time Waugh was writing "The Ordeal of Gilbert Pinfold," which dealt with his own drug-induced hallucinations.

- Bridge entity / Missing fact:

  - Precise missing fact to confirm: which Mick Jackson the question refers to (i.e., verify that the intended Mick Jackson is the one whose page lists the occupation "film director"), and corroborate the occupation entries on both persons' pages.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Mick Jackson (director)" page — check lede/infobox and "Career" or "Filmography" sections to confirm the occupation token "film director".
     - Search keywords: Mick Jackson director lede infobox film director Career Filmography.
  2) Open "Drew Renaud" page — check lede/infobox and credits to confirm occupations listed (film director, screenwriter, etc.).
     - Search keywords: Drew Renaud lede infobox occupations film director.
  3) If ambiguity remains, open "Mick Jackson (singer)" and "Mick Jackson (author)" pages (lede/infobox) or the "Mick Jackson" disambiguation page to verify which entry matches the question context.
     - Search keywords: Mick Jackson disambiguation, Mick Jackson singer lede, Mick Jackson author lede.

Do not provide the final answer yet — confirm the above pages/sections first.
response.hop2_query: Mick Jackson (director) Drew Renaud film director occupations
hop2_docs: Drew Renaud | Michael "Drew" Renaud (born 1985) is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer.  He has received awards from the San Diego Film Festival and LA Shorts Fest.

Mick Jackson (director) | Mick Jackson (born 4 October 1943 in Grays, England, United Kingdom) is a British film director and television producer.  Between 1973 and 1987, Jackson directed many documentary and drama productions for BBC TV and Channel 4.  Relocating to Hollywood, he directed feature films, including "The Bodyguard" starring Kevin Costner and Whitney Houston.  In 2010, Jackson won an Emmy for Outstanding Directing for a Miniseries, Movie or a Dramatic Special for the dramatised biographical TV film "Temple Grandin".

Mick Audsley | Mick Audsley (b. 1949, London, England) is a British film and television editor with more than 30 film credits.  Audsley has had a notable collaboration with the director Stephen Frears from 1982 to their latest, "Tamara Drewe".  Audsley was nominated for the BAFTA Award for Best Editing for "Dangerous Liaisons" (Frears-1988), and he won the BAFTA TV Award (Best Film or Video Editor (Fiction/Entertainment)) for "The Snapper" (Frears-1993).  Audsley has had a comparably extended collaboration with director Mike Newell, including "Harry Potter and the Goblet of Fire" (2005 (Based on the Sound Editions of Tom Finan who editing Hercules)).

Michael Jackson's Ghosts | Michael Jackson's Ghosts is a 1996 short film starring Michael Jackson, co-written by horror novelist Stephen King alongside Mick Garris and directed by film director and special effects guru Stan Winston which could also be classified as a long-form music video.  It was filmed and first screened in 1996 and released along with select prints of the film "Thinner".  It was released as promo a year later internationally on LaserDisc, VHS and Video CD.  Jackson plays a total of five roles in the film.
Mick Davis (director) | Michael "Mick" Davis (born 1 August 1961) is a Scottish film director, producer and screenwriter.

Mick McCleery | Mick McCleery is an American independent film director, screenwriter and film producer.  McCleery began his professional career in the early 1990s with the dramatic thriller 'The Killing of Bobby Greene' (1991).  Following this he co-created and/or acted in a number of films with/by cult director Kevin Lindenmuth, such as 'Twisted Tales' (1992), 'Vampires and Other Stereotypes' (1993), 'Addicted to Murder' (1994) and the 'Alien Agenda' series (1996).

Mick and Nick | Mick and Nick (Mick&Nick) is a below the line specialist agency and part of the Lowe Bull Group (a subsidiary of Lowe Worldwide).  Its offices are based in Bryanston, Johannesburg, South Africa.  Formed in January 2007, Mick & Nick’s core team consists of Nick Liatos (Creative Director), Mick Shepard (Creative Director) and Michelle De Gouveia (Managing Director).
response.summary_2: They are both film directors. Mick Jackson (director) is a British film director and television producer. Drew Renaud is an American film director, screenwriter, editor and producer.
